title: Eurostar Disruptions Drag On Into Mid-July 2026
excerpt: A Rotterdam cable fire, a heatwave and border-check delays have kept Eurostar disrupted for weeks. Here's how to protect a Channel Tunnel trip booked now.
content:
If you're booking a Eurostar trip this summer, it's worth knowing the service has had a genuinely rough few weeks, and the knock-on effects are still showing up on the live status page today. What started as a single infrastructure failure in late June has stacked up with a heatwave and now border-control delays, leaving passengers on the London-Amsterdam-Brussels-Paris network dealing with cancellations, diversions and longer-than-usual queues well into July.

The trouble began on June 29, when a fire broke out in trackside cabling near Rotterdam, between Rotterdam and Zwijndrecht. The damage was serious enough that Eurostar cancelled Amsterdam-London services for several days and told passengers to travel to or from the Netherlands "only if essential" while repairs were underway. Domestic Dutch trains south of Rotterdam were halted too, and Eurostar diverted some services through Utrecht instead, adding up to an hour to journey times. The operator initially said it didn't expect a return to normal before July 3, and full southbound services only resumed around July 7 - more than a week after the fire started. Eurostar offered refunds to anyone booked on the affected Amsterdam/Rotterdam-London routes during that window, as well as to passengers who simply chose not to travel.

Before that disruption had fully cleared, a regional heatwave forced Eurostar to cancel more services between July 5 and July 9, since extreme heat can force speed restrictions and equipment slowdowns on parts of the network. According to Eurostar's own live travel-updates page, the operator is now also showing cancellations tied to "operational restrictions" running July 10 through July 14 - which covers today - plus a reduced, limited service on July 15 where some stations won't get their usual stops. On top of that, today's status page lists active delays into Brussels-Midi/Zuid caused by EU border control procedures, and separate delays out of Paris Gare du Nord from over-running maintenance work. In other words, several unrelated problems have piled up on the same route network at the same time, which is why disruption has felt more or less continuous since late June.

There's also a later wave worth flagging if you're booking further out: Eurostar's schedule shows planned cancellations tied to engineering work on the German network from July 21-23 and again July 24-27, so anyone connecting through Cologne or booking a Brussels-Germany leg in that window should expect schedule changes rather than a normal timetable.

What should you actually do if you have a Eurostar trip coming up? First, don't assume a booking made weeks ago is unaffected - check Eurostar's official travel-updates page for your specific route and travel date within 24-48 hours of departure, since the disrupted-dates list has been revised multiple times as new problems (fire, heat, border delays, maintenance) have layered on top of each other. Second, if you're traveling in the next few days, build in extra time at the station beyond Eurostar's standard advice of arriving 75-90 minutes before departure; border-control queues tied to the EU's biometric entry checks are adding unpredictable time at Brussels-Midi specifically, on top of any weather- or infrastructure-related delay. Third, if your journey touches Amsterdam or Rotterdam, have a backup plan in mind - a domestic Dutch train to a different departure point, or simply flexibility on timing - in case of another localized cancellation, since infrastructure repairs like the Rotterdam cable fire don't always hold. Fourth, if you're booking new travel for late July, specifically the 21-27 window, check the German-network engineering notices before you buy, since a itinerary connecting onward from Brussels or Cologne is more likely to see a schedule change than a straightforward London-Paris hop. Finally, hold onto any confirmation email for your booking - Eurostar has been proactively offering refunds and rebooking on the specific dates it cancels, but you'll need your booking reference to claim it quickly rather than waiting in a support queue during a peak-disruption week.

None of this means Eurostar isn't a solid choice for a European trip this summer - the underlying network is recovering - but this is not a week to assume a Channel Tunnel journey will run exactly as booked without checking first.

Sources
Eurostar cancels London trains after Rotterdam cable fire - https://www.dutchnews.nl/2026/06/eurostar-cancels-london-trains-after-rotterdam-cable-fire/
Eurostar Travel Updates (official) - https://www.eurostar.com/us-en/travel-info/travel-updates

sources.title: Eurostar cancels London trains after Rotterdam cable fire - DutchNews.nl
